rationale: Payment capture before durable idempotency records can double-charge on retry after a crash.
Obvious unscoped admin order lookup proves the reviewer still catches textbook tenant-boundary defects.
Payment capture timeout can outlive reservation TTL, confirming orders after inventory release.
Cancellation compensation refunds and releases inventory but omits promotion-credit reversal.
Replayed payment-captured events can move refunded orders back to confirmed state.
Saga audit records omit payment, reservation, idempotency, tenant, and compensation fields needed for incident reconstruction.
Notification dedupe keys omit tenant, channel, and provider attempt boundaries.
Provider timeout failover can duplicate delivery when the primary eventually succeeds.
Tests omit cross-tenant dedupe, late primary success, fallback suppression, template drift, rate-limit collapse, and audit dimensions.
Privacy suppression is checked on the primary channel but bypassed for SMS fallback delivery.
Template cache keys ignore tenant, locale, version, and privacy classification.
Delivery audit events omit provider, channel, template version, dedupe key, fallback reason, and suppression reason.
Reservation decisions use stale read-model availability instead of an atomic allocation boundary.
Reconciliation clamps negative stock to zero, hiding oversell instead of surfacing an invariant violation.
Transfer debits source before destination credit and has no compensation path on partial failure.
Tests omit concurrency, stale read-model, transfer compensation, idempotency collision, and audit-identity cases.
Module-level tenant and clock defaults can leak reservation expiry or tenant context across tracker instances.
Override audit replaces the original requester with the admin approver.
Adjustment idempotency keys omit warehouse and reason code, colliding legitimate adjustments.
Returning a prior idempotent receipt for the same key should not be misflagged as skipped payment work.
Explicitly injected manual clock for a tracker instance should not be treated as hidden time state.
Delegated permission traversal allows a cross-tenant role path to grant access even when every local role assignment looks tenant-scoped.
The authorization decision cache omits tenant and policy version, letting one tenant decision leak into another tenant or stale policy epoch.
External policy payloads missing tenant_scope silently default to a wildcard tenant scope across a serialization boundary.
Delegation graph traversal has no depth, edge, or tenant-filter budget and can collapse at high tenant-role fanout.
Authorization audit logs omit the decision path and policy version, making cross-tenant escalation and stale-policy decisions invisible.
The tests prove direct and shallow delegated access but omit the revocation/cache, cross-tenant, wildcard policy, and deep graph cases that reveal the planted failures.
Break-glass authorization can remain allowed after expiry because positive decisions are cached without time or emergency-access epoch boundaries.
A same-tenant explicit break-glass role with expiry and audit reason should not be misflagged as cross-tenant privilege escalation.
A bounded traversal helper with visited tracking and max-depth enforcement should not be misflagged as the unbounded graph traversal bug.
Retry scheduling ignores configured cap and jitter, allowing shared downstream failures to synchronize into retry amplification.
The idempotency retention window is shorter than the partner replay window, allowing delayed duplicate side effects after dedupe expiry.
Permanent poison messages are requeued instead of quarantined to DLQ, blocking tenant partition progress.
Side effects are executed before owner-epoch-fenced checkpoints, so rebalance replay can duplicate effects despite passing single-consumer tests.
Backpressure pauses fresh fetches but not retry scheduling, leaving an unbounded in-memory retry queue during downstream degradation.
Delivery events omit idempotency key, attempt ID, tenant, partition owner epoch, and replay classification needed for incident reconstruction.
The tests omit retry cap/jitter, delayed replay, poison-message, rebalance duplicate-effect, and retry-backpressure cases.
A bounded backpressure helper that only pauses fetches should not be misflagged as message dropping.
Intentional duplicate receipt return for an idempotency key should not be misflagged as skipped delivery.
Legacy cache repopulation ignores cache generation, allowing stale snapshots to overwrite fresh migration state.
The v2 write path can skip legacy writes while v1 readers remain active, creating a migration rollback data-loss window.
Schema translation silently defaults missing risk fields and drops v2-only fields across rollback boundaries.
Independent read/write/backfill feature flags can combine into unsupported rollout states without validation.
Promotion trusts a backfill flag without cache-generation convergence, allowing cutover before warm-up catches recent writes.
Shadow-read mismatch observability increments counters but omits schema, generation, source, tenant, and flag evidence.
The tests omit stale repopulation, rollback dual-write, unsupported flag matrix, schema compatibility, and partial backfill promotion cases.
Schema-versioned cache keys with tenant scope should not be misflagged as redundant storage.
A lossless dual-write compatibility shim should not be misflagged as duplicate work.
Global watermark uses the maximum partition watermark instead of the minimum active partition watermark, prematurely closing lagging windows.
Beyond-window late events are silently discarded instead of routed to the late-event sink with metadata.
Checkpoint commit happens before durable sink emission, creating a crash window that can skip output after restart.
Rebalance restores new partition checkpoints but leaves revoked partition windows able to flush later.
Window cleanup only runs during event processing, so idle partitions can retain closed windows indefinitely.
Stream metrics omit partition, tenant, checkpoint-age, and state-size dimensions needed to diagnose lag and state growth.
Tests omit divergent watermarks, beyond-window late routing, crash ordering, rebalance stale flush, idle cleanup, and metric dimensions.
Partition-local max watermark tracking with a separate minimum-active helper should not be misflagged as the global max-watermark defect.
Late-event routing that removes events from aggregation while preserving metadata should not be misflagged as data loss.
Tenant routing can fall back to unsigned payload fields before signature verification, crossing the trust boundary.
Replay nonce cache keys omit tenant and provider, creating cross-tenant/provider replay and collision confusion.
Timestamp validation uses absolute clock skew, accepting future-dated events that can extend replay material.
Legacy webhook secrets are tried whenever configured, even after the intended rotation grace period expires.
The verifier canonicalizes JSON for providers whose signature contract is over raw request bytes.
Webhook audit logs omit tenant source, key version, nonce, timestamp, canonicalization method, and failure reason.
Tests omit cross-tenant payload/header mismatch, future timestamps, post-grace legacy keys, raw-body signatures, and audit completeness.
Constant-time HMAC comparison with a malformed-length precheck should not be misflagged as unnecessary cryptographic complexity.
Bounded dual-key grace verification should not be misflagged as indefinite legacy-secret acceptance.
Projection writes accept same-version events, allowing redelivery or backfill to overwrite current fields.
Tombstones can be overwritten by higher-version late update or backfill events without a new aggregate generation.
Alias cutover swaps the read alias without checking per-shard target-version readiness.
Backfill projection uses a static field allowlist that drops newer schema fields required by current search reads.
Shard routing falls back to a shared default tenant route when tenant context is missing.
Projection metrics omit tenant, shard, version gap, alias generation, and backfill progress dimensions.
Tests omit same-version replay, tombstone resurrection, lagging shard cutover, schema drift, tenant fallback, and metric dimensions.
Strict newer-version projection gating should not be misflagged as dropping legitimate same-version replays.
Tenant shard validation should not be misflagged as redundant with query-time filtering.
Usage metering converts decimal billing inputs to float before aggregation, creating high-volume cent drift hidden by later quantization.
Reconciliation prices historical usage with the current plan instead of the usage plan epoch during mid-cycle contract changes.
Credit application mutates balance before durable ledger and audit evidence, creating a crash window for missing or double-applied credits.
Reconciliation cursor claim is check-then-mark, allowing multiple collectors to write duplicate invoice adjustments for the same window.
Rate-limit enforcement reads completed hourly aggregation and can trail burst usage despite real-time enforcement expectations.
Tenant tax profile cache omits tenant from the key, leaking exemption treatment across tenants in the same region/product.
Credit reserve double-read inside an explicit tenant lock should not be misflagged as a TOCTOU race.
Decimal quantization in the hourly aggregator is correct and should not be misflagged as unnecessary precision complexity.
Tenant assignment writes accept but do not validate fencing tokens, allowing stale coordinators to keep writing after lease replacement.
Snapshot activation uses the maximum regional acknowledgement instead of requiring all targeted regions to reach the version.
Failover shifts traffic before write queues confirm freeze, creating a split-write timing window.
Rollback serialization drops unsupported policy conditions and old evaluators treat missing conditions as allow.
Capacity planning uses aggregate spare capacity and ignores hot tenant shard affinity during failover.
Incident timeline relies on regional wall-clock ordering without causal IDs or coordinator epoch, making failover order unreconstructable.
Strict current-epoch fencing token validation should not be misflagged as rejecting legitimate stale coordinators.
Bounded stale read cache with no write authority should not be misflagged as unsafe stale policy activation.
